title: CIL System — Advanced Mock 12 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of the United Kingdom?
questions.solution: The UK's currency is the Pound Sterling (£).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where are the headquarters of the United Nations located?
questions.solution: The UN headquarters is located in New York City, USA.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where is the headquarters of the World Health Organization (WHO)?
questions.solution: The WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is India's national space agency?
questions.solution: The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is India's space agency.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Taj Mahal is located in which city?
questions.solution: The Taj Mahal is in Agra, Uttar Pradesh, on the bank of the Yamuna.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Sun Temple at Konark is located in which state?
questions.solution: The Konark Sun Temple is in Odisha, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In which year did the Dandi (Salt) March take place?
questions.solution: Gandhi led the Dandi Salt March in 1930 during the Civil Disobedience Movement.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Quit India Movement was launched in which year?
questions.solution: The Quit India Movement began in August 1942.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who founded the Maurya Empire?
questions.solution: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 321 BCE.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: How many players are there in a cricket team on the field?
questions.solution: A cricket team fields eleven players.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Thomas Cup is associated with which sport?
questions.solution: The Thomas Cup is the premier men's international team badminton event.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Bharat Ratna is India's highest:
questions.solution: The Bharat Ratna is the highest civilian award of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution regulates monetary policy in India?
questions.solution: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is the country's central bank and monetary authority.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: GST in India was implemented from which date?
questions.solution: The Goods and Services Tax came into force on 1 July 2017.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The speed of light in vacuum is approximately:
questions.solution: Light travels at about 3 × 10^8 metres per second in vacuum.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 40 L of milk. 8 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 2 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 40 L, x = 8 L, n = 2.
**Working.** $=40\left(1-\tfrac{8}{40}\right)^{2}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 25.6$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 120 m and 200 m long move in opposite directions at 72 km/h and 48 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 120 m, L₂ = 200 m, s₁ = 72 km/h, s₂ = 48 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(72+48)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=33.33$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{120+200}{33.33}$.
**Answer.** $t=9.6$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 5000 at 8% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 5000, r = 8% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=5000\left(\tfrac{8}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 32.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 8:24 is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The hour hand sweeps 30° per hour and 0.5° per minute, the minute hand 6° per minute; take the smaller of the two angles.
**Formula.** $\theta=\left|30H-5.5M\right|$
**Given.** H = 8, M = 24.
**Working.** $=\left|30\times8-5.5\times24\right|=108^\circ$; smaller $=\min(108,360-108)$.
**Answer.** $\theta=108^\circ$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 5000 for 8 months and B invests Rs 6000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 6000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 5000×8 mo; B: Rs 6000×6 mo; profit Rs 6000.
**Working.** Ratio $=40000:36000$; share$_A=6000\times\tfrac{40000}{76000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 3158.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 60% above cost, then gives a 20% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 60%, discount d = 20%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{60}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{20}{100}\right)=1.28$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=28\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat covers 128 km downstream in 8 h and the same 128 km upstream in 16 h. Its speed in still water is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Downstream speed adds the stream; upstream subtracts it. Still-water speed is the average of the two.
**Formula.** $u=\tfrac12\left[(b+s)+(b-s)\right]$
**Given.** Downstream 128 km in 8 h; upstream 128 km in 16 h.
**Working.** Downstream $=\tfrac{128}{8}=16$ km/h, upstream $=\tfrac{128}{16}=8$ km/h.
**Answer.** Still-water speed $u=\tfrac{16+8}{2}=12$ km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 6 h, but with a leak it takes 7 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 6 h; with leak b = 7 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{6}-\tfrac1{7}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{6\times7}{7-6}$.
**Answer.** $T=42$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 80 L of milk. 20 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 3 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 80 L, x = 20 L, n = 3.
**Working.** $=80\left(1-\tfrac{20}{80}\right)^{3}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 33.75$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 110 is divided in the ratio 3 : 7, the larger part is:
questions.solution: Larger part = 110 × 7/10 = 77.

questions.stem: An item bought for Rs 600 is sold for Rs 750. The profit percentage is:

questions.stem: Successive discounts of 15% and 10% on a marked price of Rs 1500 give a final price of:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A and B invest in the ratio 4 : 5. Out of a total profit of Rs 10800, A's share is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A number divided by 8 gives quotient 13 and remainder 5. The number is:
questions.solution: Number = divisor × quotient + remainder = 8×13+5 = 109.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 10 men complete a work in 12 days, 5 men will complete it in:
questions.solution: Men-days constant: 10×12 = 5×D, D = 24 days.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 8 h and pipe B in 3 h. Together they fill it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is the average of the first 20 natural numbers?
questions.solution: Average of first n naturals = (n+1)/2 = (20+1)/2 = 10.5.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Simple interest on Rs 5000 at 10% per annum for 3 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A 200 m long train crosses a pole in 18 seconds. Its speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The average age of 5 persons is 28 years. If a person aged 38 leaves, the average of the remaining 4 is:
questions.solution: Total = 140; remaining = 140 − 38 = 102; average = 25.5.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The LCM of 9 and 12 is:
questions.solution: The lowest common multiple of 9 and 12 is 36.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An article sold at Rs 720 incurs a 10% loss. Its cost price is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The compound ratio of 4 : 7 and 4 : 7 is:
questions.solution: Compound ratio = (4×4) : (7×7) = 16 : 49.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The square root of 4225 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The percentage increase when a value rises from 60 to 84 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 3 km towards north, then turns east and walks 4 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(3² + 4²) = √25 = 5 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'PEN' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: PEN → RGP.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All pens are books. All books are tables. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: All pens are tables.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'GEM'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of G, E, M to get 25.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 5, 11, 23, 47, 95, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 95 × 2 + 1 = 191.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the brother of B. B is the brother of C. C is the father of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Uncle.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 6 km towards north, then turns east and walks 8 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(6² + 8²) = √100 = 10 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: No A is B. All B are C. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: Some C are not A.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 5, 10, 20, 40, ?
questions.solution: Each term is multiplied by 2, so next = 40 × 2 = 80.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 6 km north, then 8 km east. His distance from the start is:
questions.solution: Resultant = √(6² + 8²) = 10 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 4, 9, 16, 25, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 6² = 36.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: Copper, Iron, Oxygen, Zinc?
questions.solution: Oxygen belongs to a different category from the rest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Dog : Kennel :: Horse : ?
questions.solution: Just as Kennel relates to Dog, Stable relates to Horse.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 1 January is a Thursday, what day is 8 January?
questions.solution: 8 − 1 = 7 days, exactly one week, so it is again Thursday.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 20 → 10.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 18 people, X is 8th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 18 − 8 + 1 = 11.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: Z, X, V, T, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is R.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If each letter is replaced by the next letter, FISH is coded as:
questions.solution: Shift each letter forward by one to get GJTI.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A=1, B=2, ... then the sum of the letters of ACE is:
questions.solution: Add the alphabet positions of A, C, E to get 9.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: The only daughter of her mother is the woman herself, so she is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: All roses are flowers. All flowers fade. Which conclusion follows?
questions.solution: Both premises are universal affirmatives, so the chained conclusion 'All roses fade' follows.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: M, N, O, P, ?
questions.solution: Consecutive alphabet letters; after P comes Q.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: The angle between the hands of a clock at 4:00 is:
questions.solution: Each hour = 30°; at 4:00 the hands are 4 × 30 = 120° apart.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct meaning of the word 'Frugal'.
questions.solution: 'Frugal' means careful with money, i.e. economical.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Abundant'.
questions.solution: 'Abundant' means existing in large quantity, i.e. plentiful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Brief'.
questions.solution: 'Brief' means short or concise.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Happy'.
questions.solution: 'Happy' is synonymous with 'joyful'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Enormous'.
questions.solution: 'Enormous' means very large, i.e. huge.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Diligent'.
questions.solution: 'Diligent' describes someone hard-working and careful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Candid'.
questions.solution: 'Candid' means open and frank in speech.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Vivid'.
questions.solution: 'Vivid' means bright, clear and intense.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Ancient'.
questions.solution: 'Ancient' (very old) is opposite to 'modern'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Victory'.
questions.solution: 'Victory' is opposite to 'defeat'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Expand'.
questions.solution: 'Expand' is opposite to 'contract'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Generous'.
questions.solution: 'Generous' is opposite to 'stingy' (mean with money).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Transparent'.
questions.solution: 'Transparent' (see-through) is opposite to 'opaque'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'Optimist'.
questions.solution: An optimist's opposite is a 'pessimist'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correctly spelt word.
questions.solution: The correct spelling is 'Occasion' (one c, two s).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She is good ___ mathematics.'
questions.solution: The idiom is 'good at' a subject or skill.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He has been living here ___ 2010.'
questions.solution: 'Since' is used with a point of time like a year.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Divide this ___ two parts.'
questions.solution: 'Divide into' is the correct collocation for separation.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is married ___ a doctor.'
questions.solution: The correct collocation is 'married to' someone.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'The book is ___ the table.'
questions.solution: 'On' indicates resting on a surface.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a person who cannot read or write':
questions.solution: Such a person is 'illiterate'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The study of living organisms is called:
questions.solution: 'Biology' is the study of all living organisms.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One who studies stars and celestial bodies is an:
questions.solution: An 'astronomer' scientifically studies celestial bodies.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a speech made without preparation':
questions.solution: An unprepared speech is delivered 'extempore'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: A place where birds are kept is called:
questions.solution: An 'aviary' houses birds; an apiary keeps bees.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block can be placed in:
questions.solution: Direct mapping fixes each block to one line (block mod #lines), unlike associative caches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /26 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 26 = 6; usable = 2^6 − 2 = 62.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 101₂ is:
questions.solution: 101₂ = 5₁₀ (sum of place values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A max-heap is a complete binary tree in which each parent is:
options: Greater than or equal to its children
questions.solution: In a max-heap every parent ≥ its children, putting the maximum at the root.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 40), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 7, 15, 20, 40, 55.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 5-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) guarantees:
questions.solution: 2PL (growing then shrinking phase) ensures serializability, though deadlocks are still possible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 3 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^3 = 8; host bits = 5 ⇒ 2^5 − 2 = 30 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For every nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) there exists:
questions.solution: NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same (regular) languages — the subset construction proves it.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.95 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average-case time to search a well-distributed hash table is:
questions.solution: With a good hash function and low load factor, lookup is expected constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /27 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 27 = 5; usable = 2^5 − 2 = 30.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about processes and threads is INCORRECT?
options: Threads of the same process have separate heaps
Context switching between threads is cheaper than between processes
Each thread has its own stack
Threads share the process's code and data
questions.solution: Threads share the heap; only stacks and registers are per-thread.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 20, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting a node at the front of a singly linked list takes time:
questions.solution: Only a couple of pointer updates are needed — constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:
questions.solution: HTTP uses port 80; HTTPS uses 443.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11001000 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 200 − 256 = -56.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree visits the keys in:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST yields keys in ascending order.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: IP addresses at the network layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
MAC addresses at the data-link layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×20 array of 4-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 200×20×4 = 16000 B = 15.63 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 111₂ is:
questions.solution: 111₂ = 7₁₀ (sum of place values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accessing the i-th element of an array by index takes time:
questions.solution: Arrays support constant-time random access via base address + index×size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Has a completely separate address space
Has its own copy of global data
Shares the address space of its process
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 8, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Translates the whole program before execution
Executes line by line at run time
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as XOR.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 170 150" width="170" height="150"><text x="26" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">A</text><text x="66" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">B</text><text x="112" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">Y</text><line x1="20" y1="40" x2="150" y2="40" stroke="#475569"/><text x="30" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="55" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="75" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="75" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="95" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="95" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="115" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text></svg>
figure.caption: Truth table

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 4-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 4 bits give 2^4 = 16 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 6 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 2 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^2 = 4; host bits = 6 ⇒ 2^6 − 2 = 62 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">XOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: XOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case lookup time of a hash table (all keys colliding) is:
questions.solution: If every key hashes to the same bucket, lookup degrades to scanning a list — O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(6) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 13, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which condition is NOT one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock needs mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, NO preemption, and circular wait — so 'preemption' is not a condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 64K memory locations?
questions.solution: 64K = 65536 = 2^16, so 16 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The base (radix) of the hexadecimal number system is:
questions.solution: Hexadecimal uses 16 symbols (0–9, A–F).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 50), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 20, 25, 33, 50, 70.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">70</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">20</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">33</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
questions.solution: ACID guarantees reliable transaction processing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 10 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 10×9/2 = 45 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 16K memory locations?
questions.solution: 16K = 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about keys is INCORRECT?
options: A table can have multiple candidate keys
A primary key may contain null values
A foreign key can be null
questions.solution: A primary key must be non-null and unique.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 140" width="220" height="140"><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="40" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="160" y1="30" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="110" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">A</text><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">B</text><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">C</text><circle cx="160" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">D</text></svg>
figure.caption: Undirected graph (4 nodes)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP connection is established using a:
questions.solution: TCP setup uses SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; teardown uses a four-way exchange.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 64 elements needs at most how many comparisons?
questions.solution: Worst-case comparisons ≈ ⌈log₂ 64⌉ = 6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly — more frames causing more page faults — can occur with which replacement policy?
questions.solution: FIFO can exhibit Belady's anomaly; stack algorithms like LRU and OPT cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NAND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NAND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 100 slots holds 30 keys. Its load factor is:
questions.solution: Load factor α = n/m = 30/100 = 0.3.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 10000010 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 130 − 256 = -126.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: Solvable in polynomial time for sure
In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 2 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 2KB = 2^11 bytes, so the offset field is 11 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which sorting algorithm is in-place (O(1) extra space)?
questions.solution: Quicksort sorts within the array; standard merge sort needs O(n) auxiliary space.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For large n, which growth ordering is correct?
options: log n grows faster than n
questions.solution: Exponential 2ⁿ eventually dominates any polynomial like n².

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 100 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 1Mbps×100ms ÷ 8 ≈ 12.21 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Given the network 192.168.1.0/26, the number of usable host addresses per subnet is:
questions.solution: /26 leaves 6 host bits ⇒ 2⁶ − 2 = 62 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 64 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 64 bits ÷ 8 = 8 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock requires NO preemption; allowing preemption actually prevents deadlock.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 8 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.002×8ms ≈ 16099.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/250 = 0.4 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a 5-stage instruction pipeline, a data hazard arises when:
options: An instruction needs a result not yet produced by an earlier instruction
questions.solution: Data hazards occur from dependencies; they are mitigated by forwarding or stalls.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 12 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 10-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 10 bits give 2^10 = 1024 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up both reads and writes equally
Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number FF₁₆ is:
questions.solution: FF₁₆ = 255₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about TCP and UDP is INCORRECT?
options: UDP has lower overhead than TCP
questions.solution: UDP is best-effort with no ordering or reliability guarantees.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 60), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 40, 45, 50, 60, 85.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">60</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">45</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">85</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree
